content.problemStatement: Write a C program that takes two floating-point numbers as input and calculates their product. Print the result with two decimal places.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two floating-point numbers num1 and num2 separated by a single space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a line in the format "Product: X" where X is the product of num1 and num2 rounded to exactly two decimal places.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Multiplying 0.2 by 2.1 gives 0.42, which already has two decimal places.
10.5 multiplied by 2.0 equals 21.00 after formatting to two decimal places.

solution.approach: For each test case we need the product of two real numbers and to print it with two decimal places using half‑up rounding. The straightforward way is to read the numbers, multiply them and format the result. To avoid binary floating‑point rounding issues we use java.math.BigDecimal which can represent the input exactly and provides a setScale method with RoundingMode.HALF_UP. This guarantees the output matches the required rounding rule. The algorithm runs in O(T) time and O(1) extra space besides the input strings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time multiplication per test case.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;import java.math.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));String line=br.readLine();if(line==null||line.isEmpty())return;int T=Integer.parseInt(line.trim());StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();for(int i=0;i<T;i++){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());BigDecimal a=new BigDecimal(st.nextToken());BigDecimal b=new BigDecimal(st.nextToken());BigDecimal prod=a.multiply(b).setScale(2,RoundingMode.HALF_UP);sb.append("Product: ").append(prod.toPlainString());if(i<T-1)sb.append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
